FT_IC1.evidence: Prospectively collected data from the ABCD study analyzed from 3 time points: baseline (2016-2018, ages 9-10 years), year 1 (2017-2019), and year 2 (2018-2020). Clear U.S. ABCD population with diverse national sample.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using group-based trajectory modeling to characterize distinct patterns of social media use over time, with multiple linear regression analyses estimating associations between trajectories and cognitive performance scores.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAMA, December 2, 2025. No indication of duplication or prior publication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time (hours per day) trajectories paired with cognitive performance on multiple domains (NIH Toolbox: ORRT, PSMT, PVT, total composite). Cognitive function is individual-trait-level behavioral/cognitive variable. Social media is IV.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients reported comparing trajectory groups to reference (no/very low SM use). Low increasing SM: ORRT (−1.39 [95% CI, −2.10 to −0.68]), PSMT (−2.03 [95% CI, −2.91 to −1.15]), PVT (−2.09 [95% CI, −2.78 to −1.41]), total composite (−0.85 [95% CI, −1.30 to −0.40]).

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal; social media is the IV (predictor), not the DV. EC7 applies only when SM is DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV anywhere in the paper. This paper uses SM as IV throughout.
notes: All inclusion criteria met. Longitudinal design (ages 9-13) with clear SM trajectories and cognitive outcomes. No exclusion criteria triggered.

FT_IC1.evidence: Prospective cohort study using data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study across 21 study sites from October 2016 to October 2018. Children aged 9 to 10 years at baseline assessed across 4 waves (baseline, year 1, year 2, year 3). U.S. ABCD confirmed.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using longitudinal, cross-lagged structural equation panel models. Random-intercept cross-lagged panel model (RI-CLPM) employed to partition between-person and within-person effects, with reported autoregressive and cross-lagged paths.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open, May 21, 2025. No indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use (time spent on social media, self-reported in hours) paired with depressive symptoms (Child Behavior Checklist depressive problems score). Depressive symptoms is individual-trait-level mental health variable. Social media functions as both IV and DV in bidirectional analysis.

FT_IC3.evidence: Within-person cross-lagged associations: social media use higher than person-level mean in year 1 associated with greater depressive symptoms in year 2 (β, 0.07; 95% CI, 0.01-0.12; P = .01; medium effect size).

FT_IC4.evidence: Year 2 to year 3: social media use higher than person-level mean in year 2 positively associated with greater depressive symptoms in year 3 (β, 0.09; 95% CI, 0.04-0.14; P < .001; medium effect size). Multiple numeric results reported.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal; however, social media is analyzed as both IV and DV (bidirectional cross-lagged design). Paper includes SM-as-IV analysis (social media predicts depressive symptoms) alongside SM-as-DV analysis. Bidirectional design exempts from EC7 exclusion.
notes: All inclusion criteria met. Large diverse longitudinal sample (N=11,876 at baseline) with within-person focus clarifying directionality. Random-intercept approach isolates time-varying associations from stable individual differences. No exclusion criteria triggered.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzed prospective cohort data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (N=9,243; ages 10–11 years in Year 1 in 2017–2019; 48.8% female; 44.0% racial/ethnic minority). U.S. ABCD confirmed.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using multivariable linear regression models with robust standard errors. Generalized structural equation models with maximum likelihood estimation used to test mediation. Prospective design across three time points (Year 1, 2, 3).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology, published online February 19, 2025. No indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper reports typical daily screen time for six subtypes including social media (explicitly isolated), texting (phone-specific), and videos. Social media time and texting are separately measured and reported with numeric results. Paired with manic symptoms (7 Up Mania scale), an individual-trait-level mental health variable. Social media and texting are IVs. Separate subtype results permit evaluation of phone/SM-specific exposures apart from non-qualifying modalities (TV, generic gaming).

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted linear regression results for specific subtypes: social media (B=0.20, 95% CI 0.09, 0.32, p=0.001), texting (B=0.18, 95%CI 0.08, 0.28, p<0.001), videos (B=0.14, 95% CI 0.08, 0.19, p<0.001), video games (B=0.09, 95% CI 0.04, 0.14, p=0.001).

FT_IC4.evidence: Overall typical daily screen time in Year 1 prospectively associated with higher manic symptoms in Year 3 (B=0.05, 95% CI 0.03, 0.07, p<0.001). Mediation analysis reported indirect effects: social media → problematic SM use (Indirect effect, B=0.12, 95% CI 0.09, 0.16, p<0.001); video games → problematic video game use (Indirect effect, B=0.08, 95% CI 0.05, 0.10, p<0.001).

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (Year 1 → Year 3 follow-up); however, screen time (including social media and texting) functions as IV predicting manic symptoms as DV. Social media is not DV in any analysis; manic symptoms are the outcome. EC7 does not apply.
notes: All inclusion criteria met. Paper isolates and reports phone/SM-specific modalities (social media, texting) separately from non-qualifying screen types. Mediation analysis identifies problematic SM use and problematic video game use as partial mediators (47.7% and 58.0% respectively), plus sleep duration (9.0% mediation). No exclusion criteria triggered. Large diverse national sample (N=9,243).
